methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 4 same-sector + same-stage peers in Climate & Energy. Range: $110M - $1.8B.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Climate & Bio-Manufacturing

private.name: Watershed
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series A), post-money $1.8B

private.name: Graphyte
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series A), post-money $200M

private.name: Fourth Power
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series A), post-money $150M

private.name: Steady Energy
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series A), post-money $110M

data.narrative:
Carbyon is a Series A climate and energy technology company founded by Hans De Neve, having raised €25M+ to date. The valuation analysis draws on a comparable universe of four same-stage, same-sector peers: Watershed ($1.8B), Graphyte ($200M), Fourth Power ($150M), and Steady Energy ($110M). This peer set reflects the broader frontier-tech landscape, which comprises one public comparable (ticker: LNZA) and eight private companies across the sector.

The peer-median method is the applicable triangulation approach given data availability. The median valuation across the four selected comparables is $175M, with a range spanning $110M to $1.8B. This wide dispersion reflects heterogeneity in execution and investor appetite within early-stage climate technology, though the median provides a robust central estimate.

The triangulation supports a valuation range of $149M to $201M, centered on $175M. This range reflects uncertainty around Carbyon's positioning relative to peers on dimensions including technology maturity, market traction, and capital efficiency. The reliance on a single method limits precision; additional data on revenue multiples, unit economics, or follow-on funding patterns would strengthen conviction. The $1.8B outlier (Watershed) signals that venture investors have demonstrated willingness to price outlier risk in this cohort, though it does not materially shift the median-based estimate.
